title: Green shakshuka
subtitle: Eggs poached in spiced spinach and herb sauce
excerpt: A herb-forward variation on shakshuka: a base of spinach, courgette, spring onions, and green chilli cooked with garlic and cumin until vibrant and reduced, then eggs cracked in and simmered until the whites are just set. Finished with feta and fresh herbs. Quicker and lighter than the tomato version, and just as satisfying.
sourceNotes: Green shakshuka is a variation of the classic red tomato-based shakshuka, documented in Yotam Ottolenghi's 'Jerusalem' (2012) and in several Israeli and Levantine cookbooks. The green version uses spinach, herbs, and courgette as the base and is particularly associated with Israeli cooking. It represents the broader shakshuka tradition of poaching eggs in a flavoured sauce.

content.text: The green sauce cooks down quickly, spinach wilts to a fraction of its volume in minutes. The courgette adds substance and mild sweetness. The sauce should be reduced enough to hold the eggs without them sinking to the base: when you tilt the pan, the sauce should move slowly rather than run. If too loose, cook for a few more minutes uncovered before adding the eggs.

The eggs need to be cracked in individually to avoid breaking the yolks, and the pan should be covered for the last few minutes so the whites set in the steam. The yolks should still be runny when served. Do not overcook: the dish is best when the whites are just set and the yolks are liquid. Serve with good bread for scooping.

items.name: Coriander
items.prepNote: finely chopped, plus whole leaves to serve

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large wide pan over medium heat. Cook the {{spring-onion}} and {{garlic}} for 3 minutes. Add the {{courgette}} and cook for 5 minutes until starting to soften. Add the {{cumin-ground}} and {{chilli-flakes}} and cook for 30 seconds. Add the {{spinach}} and stir until completely wilted and reduced. Add the chopped {{coriander}} and {{parsley-flat}}. Season with salt and {{lemon}} juice. The sauce should be vibrant green and fairly thick.

Make 8 wells in the sauce and crack an {{eggs}} into each. Cover the pan and cook on medium-low heat for 4 to 5 minutes until the whites are just set but the yolks are still runny. Scatter {{feta}} over the top if using and add whole coriander leaves. Serve immediately, directly from the pan.

items.fix: Cover the pan during the final egg-cooking stage, the steam from the sauce is what sets the tops of the whites. Ensure the sauce is gently simmering before adding the eggs
items.cause: Pan not covered or heat too low
items.symptom: Egg whites do not set properly

items.fix: Cook the sauce for an extra 3 to 4 minutes uncovered before adding the eggs. Squeeze the courgette before adding to remove excess moisture
items.cause: Spinach and courgette still too wet
items.symptom: Sauce is too thin and eggs sink

items.fix: The sauce should come together in 15 minutes. Overcooked spinach turns dull grey. Add the herbs at the very end to preserve their colour
items.cause: Cooked too long or at too high heat
items.symptom: Sauce is grey-green rather than bright green

items.fix: Check at 4 minutes, the whites should be completely opaque but the yolks visibly liquid when the pan is tilted. Remove from heat and serve immediately

content.text: Green shakshuka grew up alongside its red cousin as part of Israeli home cooking. It is a lighter, more spring-suited take on the standard. The broader shakshuka tradition of poaching eggs in a sauce is pan-Levantine. It shows up in many forms across North Africa, the Levant, and Israel. The green version has spread further thanks to Yotam Ottolenghi, who brought Israeli food culture to a wider audience. It does the same job as the red version: a whole meal from one pan, with the number of eggs scaled to fit the table.
